meta.title: X150 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar XK X150 engine — 4.2-litre AJ34, 5.0-litre AJ133, XKR supercharged. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XK X150 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X150 engine typically costs £3,500–£11,000 supply only [JAG-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the 4.2-litre AJ34 sits at £3,500–£6,000, the 5.0-litre AJ133 at £4,000–£6,500, and the supercharged XKR at £5,500–£11,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which X150 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.2-litre naturally aspirated AJ34 (XK 4.2, 2005–2009) is the X150's most dependable engine — no supercharger complexity, simpler timing system, and a proven track record from the X100. The 5.0-litre naturally aspirated AJ133 is also robust but watch for early timing chain guide wear on pre-2012 cars.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XK X150?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early 5.0-litre AJ133 engines suffer from timing chain guide wear (pre-2012) — an engine-out job costing £2,500–£4,500. The 4.2-litre AJ34 and AJ34S have cooling system issues — thermostat housing and water pump failures are common. Supercharger coupler wear affects all XKR variants, causing a rattling noise on overrun. Electrical gremlins are often caused by a weak battery.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XK X150 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and year. The 4.2-litre naturally aspirated AJ34 is genuinely robust — many examples exceed 150,000 miles. The 5.0-litre AJ133 is generally reliable but pre-2012 examples need timing chain work. The supercharged variants add complexity — cooling systems and supercharger couplers are the primary concerns. Regular maintenance is critical; these are high-performance, high-maintenance grand tourers.

mainEntity.name: Which X150 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-2012 5.0-litre supercharged AJ133 (XKR, XKR-S) without documented timing chain work — the aluminium guide wear issue is well-documented and expensive to fix. A neglected 4.2-litre XKR without cooling system history is also high-risk. Avoid any engine without full service history. The XKR-S GT is rare and collectable but parts are scarce and running costs are astronomical.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X150 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle on cold start (5.0 AJ133), coolant loss or overheating (4.2 AJ34/AJ34S), rattling from the supercharger on overrun (XKR), or persistent warning lights caused by low battery voltage are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an X150 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £20,000 XKR, a £5,500–£9,000 recon is 28–45% of the car's value — well within our 60% Jaguar threshold. Against a £10,000 4.2 XK, a £3,500–£6,000 recon is 35–60% — borderline but often viable for a well-kept example. For a £35,000 XKR-S, a £6,000–£10,000 recon is 17–29% — a straightforward repair decision. Value the car and its condition first; if it's a pre-2012 5.0 without chain history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

mainEntity.name: How long do X150 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.2-litre naturally aspirated AJ34 can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The 5.0-litre AJ133 is capable of high mileage but timing chain guides are the limiting factor — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 120,000+ miles. Supercharged variants require more attention to cooling systems and supercharger couplers but are fundamentally robust.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the XK, XKR, XKR-S and XKR-S GT?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the X150 chassis. The XK uses a naturally aspirated V8 (4.2-litre AJ34, 296–305 bhp; or 5.0-litre AJ133, 385 bhp). The XKR uses a supercharged V8 (4.2-litre AJ34S, 396–416 bhp; or 5.0-litre AJ133, 510 bhp). The XKR-S (530 bhp) and XKR-S GT (550 bhp) are higher-output versions of the 5.0-litre supercharged engine, with the GT being a limited-production track-focused variant. Running costs increase significantly with each step up.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my X150 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For the 4.2-litre, look for AJ34 (naturally aspirated) or AJ34S (supercharged). For the 5.0-litre, it's AJ133 / 508PN (naturally aspirated, 385 bhp) or AJ133 / 508PS (supercharged, 510–550 bhp). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Jaguar XK X150 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X150 grand tourer — from the 4.2-litre AJ34 V8 and supercharged XKR to the 5.0-litre AJ133 that transformed this generation. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for Jaguar's last V8-powered XK. Backed by real UK enquiries [JAG-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XK X150 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X150 specifically — including the 4.2-litre AJ34 (naturally aspirated and supercharged) and the 5.0-litre AJ-V8 Gen III (AJ133) that arrived with the 2009 facelift.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any X150 engine family. The naturally aspirated 4.2 AJ34 and 5.0 AJ133 carry the strongest reliability reputations based on specialist consensus. The supercharged variants (AJ34S, 508PS) share the same fundamental architecture but add supercharger complexity — cooling systems, supercharger couplers, and timing chain tensioners are the primary concerns. The 5.0-litre AJ133 is known for timing chain guide wear on earlier examples, with updated tensioners on later cars. The 4.2-litre engine is considered more robust overall.

overview.intro: The X150 is the second and final generation of the Jaguar XK grand tourer, launched at the 2005 Frankfurt Motor Show. It succeeded the X100 and was the last V8-powered XK before the F-Type replaced it in 2014. Designed by Ian Callum, the X150 introduced an all-aluminium monocoque construction that was significantly lighter and stiffer than its predecessor. The engine line‑up was exclusively V8 petrol: the 4.2-litre AJ34 (naturally aspirated and supercharged) was carried over from the X100, replaced in 2009 by the 5.0-litre AJ-V8 Gen III (AJ133) in both naturally aspirated and supercharged forms. A 2009 facelift brought updated styling and the new engine range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Jaguar X150 (all-aluminium monocoque, front-mid-engine, rear-wheel drive) • Predecessor: Jaguar XK (X100) • Successor: Jaguar F-Type • Sibling body styles in this generation: 3-door fastback coupé, 2-door convertible
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The XK nameplate generated significant UK engine enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], with the 5.0-litre AJ133 representing the majority of X150-related demand based on market patterns.

items.quote: Timing chain guide wear is a well‑documented issue on early 5.0-litre AJ133 engines — the aluminium guide rails wear against steel tensioners, causing chain slack and potentially catastrophic failure. Later cars received updated tensioners.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented timing chain work or post‑2012 cars.

items.quote: Timing chain replacement on the 5.0-litre AJ133 is an engine‑out job requiring specialist tooling — £2,500–£4,500 is typical. A full engine replacement can exceed £9,000. The XKR-S and XKR-S GT are even more costly due to parts scarcity.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want the ultimate XK.

items.quote: The 4.2-litre supercharged XKR delivers 396–416 bhp and is cheaper to buy than the 5.0-litre XKR. The supercharger adds complexity, but the 4.2's timing system is more robust than the 5.0's.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want XKR performance on a tighter budget.

items.quote: Most buyers gravitate to the XKR, but the 385 bhp 5.0-litre naturally aspirated car is genuinely fast enough for the road — and it's cheaper to run, insure, and repair than the supercharged cars.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern XK performance without supercharger running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Good Value — more power, more complexity
Best Modern Choice — 385 bhp, no SC
High Power, High Risk — budget for chain work
Ultimate XK — very rare, very expensive

economicsRule.title: The X150 rule
economicsRule.text: A £5,500–£9,000 5.0 XKR recon against a £15,000–£25,000 XKR sits at 22–60% of the car's value — at the upper end, that brushes our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold. Against a £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to 28–45% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. For a 4.2 XK at £10,000, a £3,500–£6,000 recon is 35–60% — borderline but often viable for a well‑kept example. Value the car first; if it's a pre‑2012 5.0 without timing chain history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X150 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.2 AJ34 (naturally aspirated) — simple, robust, no supercharger complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X150 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 5.0 XKR-S GT — timing chain work is engine‑out, parts are scarce, and running costs are eye‑watering.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X150 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 5.0 AJ133 (naturally aspirated, 385 bhp) — modern power, no supercharger, cheaper to buy and run than the XKR.

problems.title: 5.0 AJ133 Timing Chain Guide Wear
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre‑2012 5.0-litre X150, look for documented timing chain work or budget £2,500–£4,500 immediately. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £5,500+ replacement that follows a failure. On a car worth under £15,000, evaluate carefully before committing.

problems.title: 4.2 AJ34 / AJ34S Cooling System & Thermostat Housing
problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected at every service — a £400–£800 preventative thermostat housing and water pump replacement is far cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows an overheat. On a car worth over £10,000, this is a straightforward repair decision.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear (AJ34S & AJ133 508PS)
problems.recommendation: If your XKR develops a rattling noise from the supercharger area, have the coupler inspected immediately — a £500–£1,000 coupler replacement is far cheaper than the £4,500+ supercharger rebuild that follows a failure. On a car worth over £20,000, this is a manageable cost of ownership.

problems.title: Electrical Gremlins (All X150s)
problems.recommendation: Before chasing electrical faults on an X150, check the battery voltage first. A weak battery (below 12.4V) can cause a cascade of warning lights that disappear with a new battery. If you're buying an X150, budget for a new battery immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: X150 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X150 only. The XK and XKR were available as both coupé and convertible. The XKR-S and XKR-S GT are coupé‑only, higher‑output versions of the 5.0-litre supercharged V8.
